title: Chapter 37: The Burning Touch
content: The groundskeeper's cottage shuddered under the wind.

I could hear the blizzard throwing itself against the walls — a feral, keening thing that clawed at the shutters and found every crack in the mortar. The fireplace sputtered and spat, fighting for oxygen in the thin, smoky air. Somewhere in the howl outside, Blackwood was dying. I couldn't see it from where I knelt on the kitchen floor, but I could feel it — a low, guttural roar beneath the storm, the sound of a century of stone and cruelty refusing to die quietly.

Julian was unconscious on the couch. The cot was too short for him; his legs dangled off the end, and someone — me, I think, in the blur between the fire and here — had dragged a moth-eaten blanket over his chest. His breathing was steady. Shallow, but steady. The pulse at his throat ticked along like a metronome counting out the seconds we'd stolen back from the flames.
Heath sat on the kitchen table.

Shirt off. Forearm laid bare, and the burn running along it like a river of ruined skin — red and weeping at the center, fading to an angry pink at the edges where the heat had kissed but not consumed. He hadn't made a sound when it happened. He'd walked through fire to pull Julian out, and the only evidence was this: a long, searing mark from his wrist to the crook of his elbow, and the way his jaw had locked so tight I could see the tendon standing out like a cable in his neck.

I knelt between his knees with the first-aid kit open on the floor. Gauze, antiseptic, burn cream, medical tape — the groundskeeper had been a practical man, apparently. The kit was better stocked than some hospitals I'd seen.
"Ready?" I asked.
Heath nodded once.
I dampened a pad with antiseptic and pressed it to the edge of the burn.

Heath's breath hitched. That was all. One small, controlled sound — the kind a person makes when they're practiced at not screaming. I knew that sound. I'd made it myself, more times than I could count.
"Sorry," I said.
"Don't be. Just do it."
So I did.

I worked methodically. My mother had insisted on practical skills — first aid, CPR, how to splint a fracture with a magazine and a shoelace. At the time, I'd thought it was her way of preparing me for a world that would hurt me. Now I think she was preparing me for a world where I'd have to hold someone else together. Either way, my hands were steady. I cleaned the wound in long, careful strokes, sweeping outward from the burn's edge, clearing away soot and debris. The antiseptic was sharp — that particular medicinal sting that blocks out everything else, chemical and clean. The smell filled the small kitchen, layered over woodsmoke and something faintly metallic underneath. Blood, maybe. Or the ghost of it.
Heath watched me.
Not his arm. Not the wound. My face.

I could feel his gaze like a second burn — lower intensity, longer exposure. I kept my attention on his forearm. Red skin. Blisters forming at the margins. The kind of wound that would scar if it wasn't cared for, and would scar anyway, probably, because some things mark you no matter how gently you treat them.

I squeezed burn cream onto my fingers. It was cool and thick, almost gelatinous, and it smelled like a hospital — aloe and something synthetic underneath, the scent of modern medicine trying to pretend it could undo what fire had done. I spread it over the wound in a thin layer, and this time Heath didn't flinch. His muscles gone slack, or maybe just locked past the point of reaction. I worked the cream from wrist to elbow, coating the worst of it, feeling the heat radiating off his skin even through the ointment.
Then something changed.

I finished the medical part — the cream applied, the gauze ready to wrap — and my hands didn't stop. They should have. The clinical work was done. But my fingers, which had been so steady, so purposeful, drifted past the edge of the burn and traced the unmarked skin beside it. The smooth, undamaged terrain of his inner forearm. The delicate blue veins visible beneath the surface. A smaller scar, older, silvery — a line so faint I might have imagined it, running parallel to the new burn like an echo.
I traced that scar with my fingertip.

And then I traced another. And another. The landscape of his skin, scarred and whole, mapped under my touch like I was reading braille. My fingertips moved up his forearm, past the bandage I hadn't yet wrapped, over the ridge of his elbow, along the hard line of his bicep. Not medical. Not necessary. Just — feeling him. The warmth of his skin. The texture of it, smooth where the scars weren't, rough where they were. The fine dark hair on his arm, flattened by the cream on my fingers.
Heath didn't move.
He watched my face, and I could feel what he was seeing: the way my expression had shifted from focused to something else. Something unguarded. Something I didn't have a strategy for.
I pulled my hands back and reached for the gauze.
The silence held for the length of the wrapping. I wound it around his forearm three times, four, tucking the end neatly, securing it with tape. Professional. Finished.
Then Heath spoke.
"You didn't have to make me go in there." His voice was hoarse — smoke, cold, the effort of carrying Julian's dead weight through a burning corridor. "You could have let him burn."
I kept my eyes on the bandage. "I know."
"So why didn't you?"

The fire crackled. Outside, the wind screamed. Julian breathed his steady, unconscious breath on the couch. And I sat there with gauze residue clinging to my fingers, trying to find an answer that wasn't a lie.
"Because," I said, finally, "if I let him burn, I'd become my father."
The words landed in the quiet like stones dropped into still water. I heard them as if someone else had said them — measured them, turned them over, found them true.
"And I'd rather burn myself than be him."

It was the most moral statement I'd ever made. And it came from self-interest, not principle. I didn't save Julian because it was right. I saved Julian because the alternative was a transformation I couldn't survive — a final, irreversible convergence with the man who'd made me. Even my goodness was strategic. Even my mercy was a kind of self-defense.
But it was real.

That was the thing I couldn't escape. The motive didn't negate the act. I'd chosen to save a life. The reasons were tangled, ugly, selfish — and the choice was still mine. Still real. Still something I couldn't take back.
Heath was quiet for a long time. Then he reached out and took my hand.

Not the scarred one. Not the pact hand, with its pale ridge of healed skin from the night we'd sealed our conspiracy in blood. My other hand — my real hand, the one that still had burn cream under the fingernails. He took it and held it, fingers threading through mine, and the gesture was so simple, so utterly without performance, that I didn't know what to do with it.
"We did it," he said. "We burned it down. What now?"

I looked at him. At his bare chest rising and falling, at the bandage on his arm, at the soot still smudged along his jaw. At the firelight painting him in amber and shadow, the warm tones of wood and ash.
"Now we figure out who we are without an enemy."
His mouth twitched — not quite a smile, but the ghost of one. "That scares you more than the fire did."
"Of course it does." The words came out before I could shape them. "Enemies are easy. Peace is the hard part."

And it was true. Give me a war, and I knew exactly who I was: strategist, conspirator, the girl with the match and the plan. Give me a battle, and every choice was sharpened to a killing edge, every instinct honed, every breath purposeful. But peace? Peace was shapeless. Peace was the blank page after the last chapter, the empty room after the furniture is sold. Peace asked you to build something instead of destroy something, and I had no blueprint for that. I'd never needed one before.

Heath's thumb moved across the back of my hand. A small, absent motion — he might not have been aware of it. But I was aware of it. I was aware of every point of contact between us: his fingers laced through mine, my knees bracketing his legs where I still knelt between them, the warmth radiating off his bare skin.
I let go of his hand.
And I reached up and touched his face.

Not the pact hand. Not a calculated move. Not the brush of fingers that meant *I need something from you* or *someone's watching* or *remember our arrangement.* My right hand, the one that had just wrapped his wound, still faintly sticky with cream. My palm against his jaw. My thumb tracing the high, sharp ridge of his cheekbone. My fingers curving along the side of his neck, where the pulse beat fast and unsteady.
Just touch.
His eyes closed.
He leaned into my palm.

His whole body shifted — barely, just a fraction of an inch — tilting toward my hand like a plant toward light. The weight of his face pressed into my palm, and I felt the roughness of his stubble, the warmth of his skin, the faint dampness where melting snow had caught in his hairline. I felt the architecture of his face: the hollow beneath his cheekbone, the tendon in his neck standing taut, the corner of his jaw where it hinges. I mapped it all with my fingertips, slowly, because I'd never been allowed to do this before.
Not by him. By me.

Every touch before this had an agenda. The pact — a bloody handshake sealing a deal. The performance — his hand on my waist while the society watched. The conspiracy — shoulders pressed together in dark corners, mouths near ears, words passed like contraband. Every point of contact we'd ever shared was a transaction. A means to an end. A move on the board.
This one had nothing behind it but want.
And it was terrifying.

Because want didn't have a countermove. Want didn't have contingencies. Want was a door swinging open onto a room I'd never been in, and I couldn't see the walls or the floor or the exit, and I was stepping through it anyway because the alternative — standing in the doorway, calculating — had become unbearable.

My thumb traced his cheekbone again. His lashes were dark against his skin. His breath was warm on my wrist. He looked — not peaceful. Not relaxed. Surrendered. Like he'd been holding something tight for so long that letting go was its own kind of violence, and he was doing it anyway, letting me hold the pieces he'd been clutching.
The fire crackled. The blizzard howled. The cottage groaned.
And I knelt there with my hand on Heath Cruz's face and felt, for the first time in my life, what it meant to touch someone without wanting anything from them except the fact of their existence.
He opened his eyes.

They were dark in the firelight, the brown almost black, and they held mine the way his hand had held mine — simply, without agenda. He looked at me like he was seeing something he hadn't expected to survive long enough to witness.
I didn't move my hand. I couldn't. It was pinned there by the weight of everything we'd done and everything we hadn't yet learned to be.

Heath reached up with his good arm — his left, the uninjured one — and wrapped it around my waist. Not pulling. Just holding. His hand splayed across the small of my back, warm and broad, and even through the damp fabric of my shirt I could feel the pressure of each finger like a separate point of contact, five small fires of their own.
He drew me toward him.
And I went.

Not because I'd calculated the positioning. Not because the optics served me or the alliance demanded it or the conspiracy required another layer of entanglement. I went because I was tired. Tired of calculating. Tired of measuring distances and anticipating outcomes and treating my own body like a piece on the board. I went because he was warm and I was cold and the cottage was small and the world outside was burning and I just — I just went.

I ended up against his chest, my ear pressed to the spot below his collarbone where his heart knocked against his ribs. Fast, faster than his calm expression had let on. He was as scared as I was. He was just better at hiding it. Or maybe he wasn't hiding it at all — maybe I'd just never been close enough to feel it.

His arm tightened around me. Just enough. Just enough to be held without being trapped, and the difference between those two things was a line so fine I'd spent my whole life refusing to approach it. But now I was on the other side of it, and it hadn't swallowed me whole.

We sat like that. Him on the table, me against his chest, the fire turning everything amber and gold. Through the cottage window, I could see Blackwood burning. The flames were visible even through the blizzard — great billows of orange and red lighting the snow from beneath, turning the swirling white into something alien, something elemental. The storm and the fire, locked together in the sky over the ruins. The old building's silhouette was a black jaw against the flames, and I watched pieces of it collapse inward, sending up showers of sparks that the wind caught and threw like handfuls of dying stars.
It was beautiful, in the way that destruction always is.

It was also over. Not just the building. The era it represented. The system. The hierarchy. The rituals and oaths and blood debts. All of it, feeding the fire now, becoming nothing more than heat and light and ash.
I pressed closer to Heath. He pressed back. His chin came to rest on the top of my head, and I felt the rumble of his voice in his chest before I heard the words.
"Still here," he said.
It wasn't a question.
"Yeah," I said. "Still here."
The fire crackled. The wind screamed. Julian slept. And the two of us sat in the glow of everything we'd destroyed, holding on to the only thing left that wasn't ash.
We destroyed an empire tonight. And somehow, against all probability, we're still here.

That wasn't the end, though. I knew that even as I let my eyes close, even as I let myself drift in the warmth and the hold and the strange, unfamiliar safety of being touched without purpose. The end would come later — or maybe the beginning — when we had to figure out what to build from all this nothing. When the adrenaline faded and the burns healed and the world stopped being a simple binary of enemy and ally. When we had to face each other without the architecture of war holding us up.
But that was later.
Right now, there was just the fire. The storm. The cottage. His heartbeat under my ear, steady and sure.
Right now, that was enough.
We were still here. The question was: what do we build from the ashes?
